skill.name: orchestration
skill.description: Guides AI assistants in routing work across Claude, Codex, Gemini, Cursor, and related command-line agents.
skill.summary: Coordinate multi-provider AI agent routing, fallback handling, and result collection from external CLIs.
skill.author: Consiliency

security_audit.summary: Static command and filesystem detections are mostly Markdown examples rather than executable code, so many pattern matches are false positives. However, the skill intentionally teaches broad external CLI delegation with pre-approved Bash permissions and auto-approval flags, including write-mode examples. No evidence found of malicious exfiltration or prompt injection that attempts to override this audit, but publication should wait for safer defaults and stronger warnings.

high_findings.title: Broad External CLI Execution Permissions
high_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The skill tells users to allow Bash access for codex, gemini, and cursor-agent commands. These broad permissions let delegated agents execute external CLIs from the host environment, which can run provider code, access local project context, and consume authenticated accounts.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly require pre-approved Bash permissions for multiple external AI CLIs. This is intentional skill behavior, not an incidental scanner match.

high_findings.title: Auto-Approved Write-Mode Agent Commands
high_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The skill documents --full-auto, --yolo, and --force patterns, including workspace-write and write-mode commands. These flags can reduce human approval and allow external AI agents to modify files or run actions with limited friction.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples directly show auto-approval flags and write-mode command patterns. The risk is semantic because operators may follow these documented commands.

medium_findings.title: Filesystem Modification and Cleanup Guidance Needs Stronger Guardrails
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The skill recommends git worktrees for isolation, but it also describes cleanup of agent-created files and write-mode merge steps. Without strict review, delegated agents could create, change, or delete files that affect the repository.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file operations are documented workflow examples, not executable code. The risk is still credible because the skill instructs operators how to perform them.

medium_findings.title: Delegated Provider Prompts Can Carry Sensitive Project Context
medium_findings.description: NEEDS_REVIEW. The skill routes user prompts and project tasks to external AI providers. No credential exfiltration was found, but sending repository context to third-party CLIs can expose private code or sensitive task details if users do not scope prompts carefully.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation clearly routes work to external providers, but the exact data shared depends on later user prompts and CLI behavior. This warrants warning rather than a malicious finding.

low_findings.title: Static Backtick Command Findings Are Mostly Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for the scanner label. The reported Ruby or shell backtick detections are Markdown code fences, inline command examples, or directory diagrams. They are not executable source in this package, although some command examples remain risky when followed by a user.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line inspection shows Markdown fences and inline documentation, not Ruby execution syntax in runnable files. The semantic command risk is captured separately in high findings.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The cited lines contain front matter, table content, or provider task examples, not cryptographic APIs or hash algorithms. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, DES, or other weak cryptography being used by executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected locations do not contain cryptographic operations. Confidence is high because the files are documentation, but the exact scanner token trigger is not visible.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal and Temp Directory Findings Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for direct traversal. Relative paths such as cookbook references and /tmp worktree examples are documentation, not unsanitized path handling code. The examples still support the medium filesystem warning because users may copy the workflows.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relevant lines are instructional paths and command examples rather than file parsing logic. The remaining risk comes from operator use, not a path traversal vulnerability in code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pre-Approved Bash Wildcards for External CLIs
dangerous_patterns.description: The permissions example allows Bash(codex:*), Bash(gemini:*), and Bash(cursor-agent:*). This pattern should be narrowed or gated by explicit user approval.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The wildcard permission pattern is directly shown in the documentation. It enables broad command execution through named external CLIs.

dangerous_patterns.title: Auto-Approval Flags in External Agent Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation uses --full-auto, --yolo, and --force in agent command examples. These flags can bypass normal approval friction and should not be marketplace defaults.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The risky flags are visible in multiple command examples. The impact depends on the external CLI and workspace permissions.

content.user_title: Coordinate AI CLI Agents Across Providers
content.value_statement: Teams need a repeatable way to send coding tasks to the right AI provider. This skill documents routing, fallback, and collection patterns for Claude, Codex, Gemini, and Cursor agents.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to choose providers based on task type, context size, sandbox needs, and speed requirements.
Documents native Task agent patterns for Codex, Gemini, Cursor, and Claude-based workflows.
Provides read-only and write-mode command examples for external AI CLIs.
Describes fallback chains for authentication failures, rate limits, and provider timeouts.
Shows result collection patterns for parallel Task and TaskOutput workflows.
Recommends git worktrees for isolating write-mode agent changes.
limitations: It is documentation only and does not provide executable orchestration code.
It assumes external CLIs are installed, authenticated, and permitted by the host tool.
Some examples use auto-approval flags that require strict operator review before use.
Model names and provider flags may become outdated as provider CLIs change.

use_cases.title: Route coding work to a specialist provider
use_cases.description: Choose Codex for sandboxed execution, Gemini for large context, or Cursor for IDE-focused edits.

use_cases.title: Run parallel agent reviews
use_cases.description: Launch several provider agents for security, architecture, and implementation review, then collect their outputs.

use_cases.title: Design provider fallback procedures
use_cases.description: Create fallback chains for authentication failures, rate limits, and timeouts during AI-assisted development.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Provider
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the orchestration skill to recommend the best provider for this task: [describe task]. Explain the reason and any safety limits.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Read-Only Delegation
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the orchestration skill to draft a read-only delegation plan for [task]. Include provider choice, command shape, and result collection steps.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Parallel Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the orchestration skill to plan parallel reviews for [repository or feature]. Assign providers by strength and define how results should be compared.

prompt_templates.title: Design Isolated Write Mode
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the orchestration skill to design a write-mode workflow for [change]. Require worktree isolation, approval checkpoints, cleanup, and rollback steps.

output_examples.input: Route a large security review with no file changes allowed.
output_examples.output: The skill would recommend a read-only provider plan, likely using Gemini for large context and Codex for sandboxed checks, with explicit no-write instructions.

output_examples.input: Plan parallel implementation reviews for a multi-file refactor.
output_examples.output: The skill would assign separate providers to architecture, implementation risk, and test coverage, then describe how to collect and reconcile results.
The skill would recommend a separate git worktree, an approved command plan, post-run diff review, and cleanup before any merge.

best_practices: Prefer read-only mode unless the user explicitly approves write access.
Use isolated git worktrees for any agent that may modify files.
Review provider commands and CLI help output before launching delegated work.
anti_patterns: Do not grant broad Bash permissions to untrusted agents without review.
Do not use auto-approval flags for write-mode tasks in a shared workspace.
Do not merge delegated agent changes without inspecting the diff and generated files.

faq.question: Does this skill run external commands by itself?
faq.answer: No. It is documentation, but it instructs users how to run external AI CLIs.

faq.question: Which tools does it mention?
faq.answer: It discusses Claude, Codex, Gemini, Cursor Agent, Task agents, and TaskOutput collection.

faq.question: Is read-only mode the default?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented default is read-only mode, with separate examples for write mode.

faq.question: Why is this skill high risk?
faq.answer: It recommends broad CLI permissions and auto-approval flags that can execute or modify code through external agents.

faq.question: Can it support parallel reviews?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents launching multiple provider agents and collecting their results asynchronously.

faq.question: How should write-mode use be controlled?
faq.answer: Use a separate git worktree, review the diff, and merge only approved changes.
